content.text: Tool input validation is the first defence between the model's output and your systems. The model is an untrusted caller: it may hallucinate argument values, misread schema constraints, or be manipulated by injected content in retrieved data. Every tool handler must independently validate every input before using it, regardless of what the JSON Schema declares.

attrs.alt: Tool input validation three layers: JSON schema, handler validation, injection guard
attrs.title: Tool Input Validation Layers

content.text: The JSON Schema the model sees is a hint. The validation logic in the handler is the control. Never trust that the model satisfied the schema constraints — validate again in code.
JSON Schema constraints in the tool definition steer the model toward valid inputs. Use

prevents the model from supplying unexpected fields, which could expose injection surface or trigger unintended code paths. It also makes the schema self-documenting — the model knows exactly which fields exist.
Pydantic models validate inputs at the handler boundary with clear, structured error messages. Define one model per tool and validate immediately on entry.
validator goes beyond the regex: even a string matching
can have an invalid month (13) or day (00). The extra check catches that without an exception in the downstream date parser.

String inputs that reach a database query, shell command, file path, or URL must be checked for injection patterns. Parameterised queries prevent SQL injection; the guards below prevent other vectors.

These guards run inside the handler before the validated value touches any backend system. They are not exhaustive — SQL injection is handled by parameterised queries at the database layer, not by regex — but they catch the most common misuse patterns early.
The model reads tool results and decides what to do next. A validation error that identifies the field and the reason allows the model to self-correct in the next turn without human intervention.
when the value is safe to echo back (no PII, no secrets). Omit it when the field might contain sensitive data like email addresses or token fragments.

Some constraints span multiple fields: a date range where end must follow start, or a limit that is only valid when a filter is also provided. These cannot be expressed in JSON Schema and must be checked in the handler.
Validation code deserves its own test suite. Test every boundary: the edge of a valid range, one step past the boundary, each invalid format, and each injection pattern.

import pytest
from mytools.orders import search_orders, export_orders
from mytools.guards import reject_path_traversal, reject_ssrf
from pathlib import Path

def test_invalid_status():
    r = search_orders(status="complete")
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert r["field"] == "status"

def test_limit_too_high():
    r = search_orders(limit=200)
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"

def test_limit_boundary_valid():
    # limit=100 is the max allowed — should not error
    r = search_orders(limit=100)
    assert "error" not in r

def test_bad_date_format():
    r = search_orders(date_from="July 1")
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert "date_from" in r["message"]

def test_date_range_inversion():
    r = export_orders(date_from="2025-06-01", date_to="2025-05-01")
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"

def test_path_traversal():
    sandbox = Path("/workspace")
    r = reject_path_traversal("../../etc/passwd", sandbox)
    assert r is not None
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"

def test_path_valid():
    sandbox = Path("/workspace")
    r = reject_path_traversal("/workspace/data/file.txt", sandbox)
    assert r is None

def test_ssrf_localhost():
    r = reject_ssrf("http://localhost:8080/admin")
    assert r is not None
    assert r["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"

def test_ssrf_public_ok():
    r = reject_ssrf("https://api.example.com/data")
    assert r is None
Validate at every layer: JSON Schema steers the model; handler code enforces the rules; injection guards block dangerous inputs before they reach backend systems.
Use Pydantic to parse and validate structured inputs — it generates structured errors with field names that the model can act on.
with the field name, a human-readable reason, and (when safe) the received value — this lets the model self-correct without user intervention.
Enforce cross-field constraints in handler code; JSON Schema cannot express them.
Guard injection vectors explicitly: path traversal (
check), SSRF (private IP/localhost block), and shell injection (metacharacter filter).
Test validation logic independently of the happy path — one test per constraint boundary is the minimum.
